{{Short description|Field of philosophical inquiry}}
{{For|the early modern meaning|Natural philosophy}}

'''Experimental philosophy''' (called '''x-phi''' for short) is an emerging field of philosophical inquiry<ref>Lackman, Jon. [http://www.slate.com/id/2137223/ The X-Philes Philosophy meets the real world], [[Slate (magazine)|''Slate'']], March 2, 2006.</ref><ref>[[Kwame Anthony Appiah|Appiah, Anthony]]. [https://www.nytimes.com/2007/12/09/magazine/09wwln-idealab-t.html?_r=1 The New New Philosophy], ''[[The New York Times]]'', December 9, 2007.</ref><ref>[[Kwame Anthony Appiah|Appiah, Anthony]]. [https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=17815241 The 'Next Big Thing' in Ideas], ''[[National Public Radio]]'', January 3, 2008.</ref><ref>Shea, Christopher. [http://chronicle.com/free/v55/i06/06b00901.htm Against Intuition] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090710064213/http://chronicle.com/free/v55/i06/06b00901.htm |date=2009-07-10 }}, ''[[Chronicle of Higher Education]]'', October 3, 2008.</ref><ref name="EdmondsDavid">[[David Edmonds (philosopher)|Edmonds, David]] and [[Nigel Warburton|Warburton, Nigel]]. [http://www.prospectmagazine.co.uk/2009/03/philosophysgreatexperiment/ Philosophy's great experiment] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090930153840/http://www.prospectmagazine.co.uk/2009/03/philosophysgreatexperiment/ |date=2009-09-30 }}, [[Prospect (magazine)|''Prospect'']], March 1, 2009</ref> that makes use of [[empirical]] data—often gathered through surveys which probe the intuitions of ordinary people—in order to inform research on [[list of philosophical questions|philosophical questions.]]<ref>[http://www.experimentalphilosophy.org/ExperimentalPhilosophy.html The Experimental Philosophy Page] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150405015929/http://www.experimentalphilosophy.org/ExperimentalPhilosophy.html |date=2015-04-05 }}</ref><ref>[[Jesse Prinz|Prinz, J.]] [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CS4DdLikfPk Experimental Philosophy], ''YouTube'' September 17, 2007.</ref> This use of empirical data is widely seen as opposed to a [[Philosophical Method|philosophical methodology]] that relies mainly on [[A priori and a posteriori|a priori]] justification, sometimes called "armchair" philosophy, by experimental philosophers.<ref>[[Joshua Knobe|Knobe, Joshua]]. [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/ExperimentalPhilosophy.pdf What is Experimental Philosophy?]. ''[[The Philosophers' Magazine]]'', (28) 2004.</ref><ref>[[Joshua Knobe|Knobe, Joshua]]. [http://www.blackwell-compass.com/subject/philosophy/article_view?parent=section&last_results=page%3D2%26volume%3Dall%26section%3Dphco-naturalistic-philosophy&sortby=date&section=phco-naturalistic-philosophy&browse_id=phco_articles_bpl050&article_id=phco_articles_bpl050 Experimental Philosophy] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110725161251/http://www.blackwell-compass.com/subject/philosophy/article_view?parent=section&last_results=page=2&volume=all&section=phco-naturalistic-philosophy&sortby=date&section=phco-naturalistic-philosophy&browse_id=phco_articles_bpl050&article_id=phco_articles_bpl050 |date=2011-07-25 }}, [http://www.blackwell-compass.com/subject/philosophy/ ''Philosophy Compass''] (2) 2007.</ref><ref name="KnobeJoshua">[[Joshua Knobe|Knobe, Joshua]]. [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/phil-significance.pdf Experimental Philosophy and Philosophical Significance], ''[[Philosophical Explorations]]'' (10) 2007.</ref>
Experimental philosophy initially began by focusing on philosophical questions related to [[#Intentional action|intentional action]], the putative conflict between free will and determinism, and causal vs. descriptive theories of [[linguistics|linguistic]] [[reference]].<ref>[[Joshua Knobe|Knobe, Joshua]]. [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/ExperimentalPhilosophy.pdf What is Experimental Philosophy?] ''[[The Philosophers' Magazine]]'' (28) 2004.</ref> However, experimental philosophy has continued to expand to new areas of [[#Areas of research|research]].

Disagreement about what experimental philosophy can accomplish is widespread. One claim is that the empirical data gathered by experimental philosophers can have an indirect effect on philosophical questions by allowing for a better understanding of the underlying psychological processes which lead to philosophical intuitions.<ref name="KnobeJoshua_a">[[Joshua Knobe|Knobe, Joshua]] and [[Shaun Nichols|Nichols, Shaun]]. [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/manifesto.pdf An Experimental Philosophy Manifesto], in Knobe & Nichols (eds.) [https://www.amazon.com/Experimental-Philosophy-Joshua-Knobe/dp/0195323262 ''Experimental Philosophy''], §2.1. 2008.</ref> Others claim that experimental philosophers are engaged in [[conceptual analysis]], but taking advantage of the rigor of quantitative research to aid in that project.<ref name="LutzSebastian">Lutz, Sebastian. [http://www.spe.ut.ee/ojs-2.2.2/index.php/spe/article/view/65/ Ideal Language Philosophy and Experiments on Intuitions] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120328064521/http://www.spe.ut.ee/ojs-2.2.2/index.php/spe/article/view/65/ |date=2012-03-28 }}. [http://www.spe.ut.ee/ Studia Philosophica Estonica] [http://www.spe.ut.ee/ojs-2.2.2/index.php/spe/issue/view/The%20Role%20of%20Intuitions%20in%20Philosophical%20Methodology 2.2. Special issue: S. Häggqvist and D. Cohnitz (eds.), The Role of Intuitions in Philosophical Methodology] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120328064530/http://www.spe.ut.ee/ojs-2.2.2/index.php/spe/issue/view/The%20Role%20of%20Intuitions%20in%20Philosophical%20Methodology |date=2012-03-28 }}, pp. 117–139. 2009</ref><ref name="SytsmaJustin">{{cite journal | last1 = Sytsma | first1 = Justin | year = 2010 | title = The proper province of philosophy: Conceptual analysis and empirical investigation | url = http://philsci-archive.pitt.edu/id/eprint/4806 | journal = Review of Philosophy and Psychology | volume = 1 | issue = 3| pages = 427–445 | doi=10.1007/s13164-010-0032-1| s2cid = 7350640 | url-access = subscription }}</ref> Finally, some work in experimental philosophy can be seen as undercutting the traditional methods and presuppositions of [[analytic philosophy]].<ref>Machery, Edouard. [http://experimentalphilosophy.typepad.com/experimental_philosophy/2007/07/index.html What are Experimental Philosophers Doing?] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071105015107/http://experimentalphilosophy.typepad.com/experimental_philosophy/2007/07/index.html |date=2007-11-05 }}. [http://experimentalphilosophy.typepad.com/ ''Experimental Philosophy'' (blog)] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070811174610/http://experimentalphilosophy.typepad.com/ |date=2007-08-11 }}, July 30, 2007.</ref> Several philosophers have offered [[#Criticisms|criticisms]] of experimental philosophy.

==History==
[[File:Experimental philosophy.jpg|thumb|First lecture in Experimental Philosophy, London 1748]]
Though, in early modern philosophy, [[natural philosophy]] was sometimes referred to as "experimental philosophy",<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Anstey | first1 = P. | last2 = Vanzo | first2 = A. | year = 2012 | title = The Origins of Early Modern Experimental Philosophy | journal = Intellectual History Review | volume = 22 | issue = 4| pages = 499–518 }}</ref> the field associated with the current sense of the term dates its origins around 2000 when a small number of students experimented with the idea of fusing philosophy to the experimental rigor of [[psychology]].

While the modern philosophical movement Experimental Philosophy began growing around 2000, there are some earlier examples, such as Hewson, 1994<ref>Hewson, C. (1994). Empirical Evidence Regarding the Folk Psychological Concept of Belief. Proceedings of the ''Sixteenth Annual Conference of the Cognitive Science Society'', 403-408. Hillsdale, New Jersey. (Atlanta, Georgia).</ref> and Naess 1938,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naess |first=Arne |title='Truth' as Conceived by Those Who Are Not Professional Philosophers |year=1938}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Chapman |first=Siobhan |date=2018-09-03 |title=The experimental and the empirical: Arne Naess' statistical approach to philosophy |journal=British Journal for the History of Philosophy |volume=26 |issue=5 |pages=961–981 |doi=10.1080/09608788.2017.1336075 |s2cid=148886287 |issn=0960-8788}}</ref> and the use of empirical methods in philosophy far predates the emergence of the recent academic field. Current experimental philosophers claim that the movement is actually a return to the methodology used by many ancient philosophers.<ref name="KnobeJoshua" /><ref name="KnobeJoshua_a" /> Further, other philosophers like [[David Hume]], [[René Descartes]] and [[John Locke]] are often held up as early models of philosophers who appealed to empirical methodology.<ref name="EdmondsDavid" /><ref>Peter Anstey, "[https://blogs.otago.ac.nz/emxphi/2010/08/is-x-phi-old-hat/ Is x-phi old hat?] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160111053129/https://blogs.otago.ac.nz/emxphi/2010/08/is-x-phi-old-hat/ |date=2016-01-11 }}", ''[https://blogs.otago.ac.nz/emxphi Early Modern Experimental Philosophy Blog]'', 30 August 2010.</ref>

==Areas of research==

===Consciousness===
The questions of what [[consciousness]] is, and what conditions are necessary for conscious thought have been the topic of a long-standing philosophical debate. Experimental philosophers have approached this question by trying to get a better grasp on how exactly people ordinarily understand consciousness. For instance, work by [[Joshua Knobe]] and [[Jesse Prinz]] (2008) suggests that people may have two different ways of understanding minds generally, and Justin Sytsma and Edouard Machery (2009) have written about the proper methodology for studying [[Folk psychology|folk intuitions about consciousness]]. Bryce Huebner, Michael Bruno, and Hagop Sarkissian (2010)<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Huebner | first1 = B. | last2 = Bruno | first2 = M. | last3 = Sarkissian | first3 = H. | year = 2010 | title = What Does the Nation of China Think about Phenomenal States? | journal = Review of Philosophy and Psychology | volume = 1 | issue = 2| pages = 225–243 | doi=10.1007/s13164-009-0009-0| s2cid = 860539 }}</ref> have further argued that the way Westerners understand consciousness differs systematically from the way that East Asians understand consciousness, while Adam Arico (2010)<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Arico | first1 = A | year = 2010 | title = Folk Psychology, Consciousness, and Context Effects | journal = Review of Philosophy and Psychology | volume = 1 | issue = 3| pages = 371–393 | doi=10.1007/s13164-010-0029-9| s2cid = 30670774 }}</ref> has offered some evidence for thinking that ordinary ascriptions of consciousness are sensitive to framing effects (such as the presence or absence of contextual information). Some of this work has been featured in the [[Online Consciousness Conference]].

Other experimental philosophers have approached the topic of consciousness by trying to uncover the cognitive processes that guide everyday attributions of conscious states. Adam Arico, Brian Fiala, Rob Goldberg, and [[Shaun Nichols]],<ref>Arico, A., Fiala, B., Goldberg, R., and Nichols, S. forthcoming. ''Mind & Language''.</ref> for instance, propose a cognitive model of mental state attribution (the AGENCY model), whereby an entity's displaying certain relatively simple features (e.g., eyes, distinctive motions, interactive behavior) triggers a disposition to attribute conscious states to that entity. Additionally, Bryce Huebner<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Huebner | first1 = B | year = 2010 | title = Commonsense Concepts of Phenomenal Consciousness: Does Anyone ''Care'' about Functional Zombies? | journal = Phenomenology and the Cognitive Sciences | volume = 9 | issue = 1| pages = 133–155 | doi=10.1007/s11097-009-9126-6| s2cid = 1363867 }}</ref> has argued that ascriptions of mental states rely on two divergent strategies: one sensitive to considerations of an entity's behavior being goal-directed; the other sensitive to considerations of personhood.

===Cultural diversity===
Following the work of [[Richard Nisbett]], which showed that there were differences in a wide range of cognitive tasks between Westerners and East Asians, Jonathan Weinberg, [[Shaun Nichols]] and [[Stephen Stich]] (2001) compared [[epistemology|epistemic]] [[Intuition (philosophy)|intuitions]] of Western college students and East Asian college students. The students were presented with a number of cases, including some [[Gettier problem|Gettier cases]], and asked to judge whether a person in the case really knew some fact or merely believed it. They found that the East Asian subjects were more likely to judge that the subjects really knew.<ref>Weinberg, J., [[Shaun Nichols|Nichols, S.]], & [[Stephen Stich|Stich, S.]] (2001). [http://ruccs.rutgers.edu/ArchiveFolder/Research%20Group/Publications/NEI/NEIPT.html Normativity and Epistemic Intuitions.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110803080118/http://ruccs.rutgers.edu/ArchiveFolder/Research%20Group/Publications/NEI/NEIPT.html |date=2011-08-03 }} ''Philosophical Topics'' 29, pp. 429–460.</ref> Later [[Edouard Machery]], Ron Mallon, Nichols and Stich performed a similar experiment concerning intuitions about the reference of proper names, using cases from [[Saul Kripke|Saul Kripke's]] ''[[Naming and Necessity]]'' (1980). Again, they found significant cultural differences. Each group of authors argued that these cultural variances undermined the philosophical project of using intuitions to create theories of knowledge or reference.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Machery | first1 = E. | author-link3 = Shaun Nichols | author-link4 = Stephen Stich | last2 = Mallon | first2 = R. | last3 = Nichols | first3 = S. | last4 = Stich | first4 = S. | year = 2004 | title = Semantics, Cross-Cultural Style | url = http://www.rci.rutgers.edu/~stich/Publications/Papers/SemanticIntuitions.pdf | journal = Cognition | volume = 92 | issue = 3 | pages = B1–B12 | doi = 10.1016/j.cognition.2003.10.003 | pmid = 15019555 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.174.5119 | s2cid = 15074526 | access-date = 2010-06-05 | archive-date = 2017-09-21 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170921213425/http://www.rci.rutgers.edu/~stich/Publications/Papers/SemanticIntuitions.pdf }}</ref> However, subsequent studies have consistently failed to replicate Weinberg et al.'s (2001) results for other Gettier cases <ref>Kim, M., & Yuan, Y. (2015). No cross-cultural differences in the Gettier car case intuition: A replication study of Weinberg et al. 2001. Episteme, 12(03), 355-361. [http://philpapers.org/rec/KIMNCD] Seyedsayamdost, H. (2015). On normativity and epistemic intuitions: Failure of replication. Episteme, 12(01), 95-116. [http://philpapers.org/rec/SEYONA-2]  {{cite journal |last=Nagel |first=J. |date=November 21, 2012 |title=Intuitions and Experiments: A Defense of the Case Method in Epistemology |url=https://philpapers.org/rec/NAGIAE |journal=[[Philosophy and Phenomenological Research]] |volume=85 |issue=3 |pages=495–527 |doi=10.1111/j.1933-1592.2012.00634.x}}</ref>  Indeed, more recent studies have actually been providing evidence for the opposite hypothesis, that people from a variety of different cultures have surprisingly similar intuitions in these cases.<ref>{{cite journal | doi = 10.1111/nous.12110 | volume=51 | issue=3 | title=Gettier Across Cultures1 | year=2015 | journal=Noûs | pages=645–664 | last1 = Machery | first1 = Edouard | last2 = Stich | first2 = Stephen | last3 = Rose | first3 = David | last4 = Chatterjee | first4 = Amita | last5 = Karasawa | first5 = Kaori | last6 = Struchiner | first6 = Noel | last7 = Sirker | first7 = Smita | last8 = Usui | first8 = Naoki | last9 = Hashimoto | first9 = Takaaki| doi-access = free }}</ref>

===Determinism and moral responsibility===
{{See also|Free will#Believing in free will}}

One area of philosophical inquiry has been concerned with whether or not a person can be morally responsible if their actions are entirely determined, e.g., by the laws of [[Newtonian physics]]. One side of the debate, the proponents of which are called '[[Incompatibilism|incompatibilists]],' argue that there is no way for people to be morally responsible for immoral acts if they could not have done otherwise. The other side of the debate argues instead that people can be morally responsible for their immoral actions even when they could not have done otherwise. People who hold this view are often referred to as '[[Compatibilism|compatibilists]].' It was generally claimed that non-philosophers were naturally incompatibilist,<ref>Nahmias, E., Morris, S., Nadelhoffer, T. & Turner, J. [http://www2.gsu.edu/~phlean/papers/Surveying_Freedom.pdf Surveying Freedom: Folk Intuitions about Free Will and Moral Responsibility] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091122173645/http://www2.gsu.edu/~phlean/papers/Surveying_Freedom.pdf |date=2009-11-22 }}. ''Philosophical Psychology'' (18) 2005 p.563</ref> that is they think that if you couldn't have done anything else, then you are not morally responsible for your action. Experimental philosophers have addressed this question by presenting people with hypothetical situations in which it is clear that a person's actions are completely determined. Then the person does something morally wrong, and people are asked if that person is morally responsible for what she or he did. Using this technique Nichols and Knobe (2007) found that "people's responses to questions about moral responsibility can vary dramatically depending on the way in which the question is formulated"<ref name="NicholsKnobe2007">{{cite journal |first1=Shaun |last1=Nichols |first2=Joshua |last2=Knobe |year=2007 |title=Moral Responsibility and Determinism: The Cognitive Science of Folk Intuitions |journal=Noûs |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=663–685 |url=http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/Nichols-Knobe.pdf |doi=10.1111/j.1468-0068.2007.00666.x|citeseerx=10.1.1.175.1091 }} (PDF p.2)</ref> and argue that "people tend to have compatiblist intuitions when they think about the problem in a more concrete, emotional way but that they tend to have incompatiblist intuitions when they think about the problem in a more abstract, cognitive way".<ref name="XPhi2007Papers">{{cite web |url=http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/xphipage.htm#2007_6 |title=X-Phi Page |editor-first=Jonathan |editor-last=Phillips |publisher=Yale |date=15 August 2010}} (§Papers on Experimental Philosophy and Metaphilosophy)</ref>

===Epistemology===
Recent work in experimental [[epistemology]] has tested the apparently empirical claims of various epistemological views. For example, research on epistemic [[contextualism]] has proceeded by conducting experiments in which ordinary people are presented with vignettes that involve a knowledge ascription.<ref name="PhelanEvidence">Phelan, M. [http://www.unc.edu/home/mphelan/Evidence.pdf Evidence that Stakes Don't Matter for Evidence] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091122155317/http://www.unc.edu/home/mphelan/Evidence.pdf |date=2009-11-22 }}</ref><ref name="Feltz">Feltz, A. & Zarpentine, C. [http://faculty.schreiner.edu/adfeltz/Papers/Know%20more.pdf Do You Know More When It Matters Less?] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100712235035/http://faculty.schreiner.edu/adfeltz/Papers/Know%20more.pdf |date=2010-07-12 }} ''Philosophical Psychology''.</ref><ref name="May">{{cite journal | doi = 10.1007/s13164-009-0014-3 | pmid=22558061 | pmc=3339025 | volume=1 | issue=2 | title=Practical Interests, Relevant Alternatives, and Knowledge Attributions: an Empirical Study | year=2010 | journal=Review of Philosophy and Psychology | pages=265–273 | last1 = May | first1 = Joshua | last2 = Sinnott-Armstrong | first2 = Walter | last3 = Hull | first3 = Jay G. | last4 = Zimmerman | first4 = Aaron}}</ref> Participants are then asked to report on the status of that knowledge ascription. The studies address contextualism by varying the context of the knowledge ascription (for example, how important it is that the agent in the vignette has accurate knowledge). Data gathered thus far show no support for what contextualism says about ordinary use of the term "knows".<ref name="PhelanEvidence" /><ref name="Feltz" /><ref name="May" /> Other work in experimental epistemology includes, among other things, the examination of moral valence on knowledge attributions (the so-called "epistemic side-effect effect"),<ref>Beebe, J. & Buckwalter, W. [http://www.acsu.buffalo.edu/~jbeebe2/Beebe%20Buckwalter%20ESEE.pdf The Epistemic Side-Effect Effect] ''[[Mind & Language]]''.</ref> of the knowing-that / knowing-how distinction,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Bengson | first1 = J. | last2 = Moffett | first2 = M. | last3 = Wright | first3 = J.C. | year = 2008| title = The Folk on Knowing How | url = http://wrightjj1.people.cofc.edu/philosophy/Folk_on_Knowing_How.pdf | journal = [[Philosophical Studies]] | volume = 142 | issue = 3| pages = 387–401 | doi=10.1007/s11098-007-9193-x| s2cid = 13183723 }}</ref> and of laypeople's intuitions about lying,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Willemsen|first1=Pascale|last2=Rutschmann|first2=Ronja|last3=Wiegmann|first3=Alex|date=2017-09-01|title=Empirically Investigating the Concept of Lying|journal=Journal of Indian Council of Philosophical Research|volume=34|issue=3|pages=591–609|doi=10.1007/s40961-017-0112-z|s2cid=148791859 |issn=2363-9962|url=http://psyarxiv.com/4t7pc/}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Wiegmann|first1=Alex|last2=Samland|first2=Jana|last3=Waldmann|first3=Michael R.|date=May 2016|title=Lying despite telling the truth|journal=Cognition|volume=150|pages=37–42|doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2016.01.017|pmid=26848734|s2cid=34286380 |issn=0010-0277}}</ref> improper assertion,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kneer|first=Markus|date=August 2018|title=The norm of assertion: Empirical data|journal=Cognition|volume=177|pages=165–171|doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2018.03.020|pmid=29684696|s2cid=206867479 |issn=0010-0277|url=https://philpapers.org/rec/KNETNO-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|date=2021-07-01|title=Should I say that? An experimental investigation of the norm of assertion|journal=Cognition|language=en|volume=212|article-number=104657|doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2021.104657|issn=0010-0277|last1=Marsili |first1=Neri |last2=Wiegmann |first2=Alex |pmid=33798949 |s2cid=232422755 |doi-access=free}}</ref> and insincerity.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Marsili|first=Neri|date=2016-01-01|title=Lying by Promising: A Study on Insincere Illocutionary Acts|journal=International Review of Pragmatics|volume=8|issue=2|pages=271–313|doi=10.1163/18773109-00802005|issn=1877-3109|url=http://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/104810/1/Marsili%20-%20Lying%20by%20promising%20-%20most%20final.pdf}}</ref>

===Intentional action===
A prominent topic in experimental philosophy is [[intention]]al [[Action (philosophy)|action]].  Work by [[Joshua Knobe]] has especially been influential. "The Knobe Effect", as it is often called, concerns an asymmetry in our judgments of whether an [[Agency (philosophy)|agent]] intentionally performed an action. It is "one of the first, most important, and most widely studied effects" in experimental philosophy.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Stich |first1=Stephen P. |last2=Machery |first2=Edouard |date=2022-01-31 |title=Demographic Differences in Philosophical Intuition: a Reply to Joshua Knobe |journal=Review of Philosophy and Psychology |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=401–434 |language=en |doi=10.1007/s13164-021-00609-7 |s2cid=246433503 |issn=1878-5166}}</ref>  Knobe (2003a) asked people to suppose that the [[CEO]] of a corporation is presented with a proposal that would, as a side effect, affect the environment.  In one version of the scenario, the effect on the environment will be negative (it will "harm" it), while in another version the effect on the environment will be positive (it will "help" it). In both cases, the CEO opts to pursue the policy and the effect does occur (the environment is harmed or helped by the policy). However, the CEO only adopts the program because he wants to raise [[Profit (accounting)|profits]]; he does not care about the effect that the action will have on the environment. Although all features of the scenarios are held constant—except for whether the side effect on the environment will be positive or negative—a majority of people judge that the CEO intentionally hurt the environment in the one case, but did not intentionally help it in the other.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Knobe |first=Joshua |date=2003-07-01 |title=Intentional action and side effects in ordinary language |journal=Analysis |volume=63 |issue=3 |pages=190–194 |doi=10.1093/analys/63.3.190 |issn=0003-2638}}</ref> Knobe ultimately argues that the effect is a reflection of a feature of the speakers' underlying concept of intentional action: broadly moral considerations affect whether we judge that an action is performed intentionally. However, his exact views have changed in response to further research.{{Citation needed|date=March 2010}}

=== Experimental jurisprudence ===
[[Experimental jurisprudence]] is an emerging topic in experimental philosophy and legal scholarship that explores the nature of legal phenomena through psychological investigations of legal concepts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Tobia |first=Kevin |date=2022 |title=Experimental Jurisprudence |url=https://chicagounbound.uchicago.edu/uclrev/vol89/iss3/3/ |journal=University of Chicago Law Review |volume=89 |issue=3 |page=735}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Sommers |first=Roseanna |date=2021-07-23 |title=Experimental jurisprudence |url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.abf0711 |journal=Science |language=en |volume=373 |issue=6553 |pages=394–395 |doi=10.1126/science.abf0711 |pmid=34437107 |bibcode=2021Sci...373..394S |s2cid=236179587 |issn=0036-8075|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Prochownik |first=Karolina Magdalena |date=2021 |title=The experimental philosophy of law: New ways, old questions, and how not to get lost |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/phc3.12791 |journal=Philosophy Compass |language=en |volume=16 |issue=12 |doi=10.1111/phc3.12791 |s2cid=244057904 |issn=1747-9991|url-access=subscription |doi-access=free }}</ref> [[Kevin Tobia|Tobia]] (2022) defines it as "scholarship that addresses jurisprudential questions with empirical data, typically data from experiments."<ref name=":1"/> The field departs from traditional analytic [[Philosophy of law|legal philosophy]] in its ambition to elucidate common intuitions in a systematic fashion. Equally, unlike research in [[legal psychology]], experimental jurisprudence emphasises the philosophical implications of its findings, notably, for questions about whether, how, and in what respects, the law's content is a matter of moral perspective. Experimental jurisprudence scholarship has argued that philosophers' appeals to the content of folk legal concepts ought to be tested empirically so that, the 'big [philosophical] cost of rely[ing]... on… a concept that is distinct from that used by folk',<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Plunkett |first=David |title=A Positivist Route for Explaining How Facts Make Law |date=2012 |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/legal-theory/article/abs/positivist-route-for-explaining-how-facts-make-law/377DF152A35C8A2498C155BF821D2D60 |journal=Legal Theory |language=en |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=139–207 |doi=10.1017/S1352325212000079 |s2cid=143438667 |issn=1469-8048|url-access=subscription }}</ref> may be allocated correctly.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Flanagan |first1=Brian |last2=Hannikainen |first2=Ivar R. |date=2022-01-02 |title=The Folk Concept of Law: Law Is Intrinsically Moral |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00048402.2020.1833953 |journal=Australasian Journal of Philosophy |language=en |volume=100 |issue=1 |pages=165–179 |doi=10.1080/00048402.2020.1833953 |s2cid=228861665 |issn=0004-8402|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Whereas some legal theorists have welcomed X-Jur's emergence,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Atiq |first=Emad |date=2022 |title=Disagreement about Law and Morality: Empirical Results and the Meta-Problem of Jurisprudence |url=https://juris.jotwell.com/disagreement-about-law-and-morality-empirical-results-and-the-meta-problem-of-jurisprudence/ |access-date=2022-09-13 |website=Jotwell |language=en-US}}</ref> others have expressed reservations about the contributions it seeks to make.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Jiménez |first=Felipe |date=2021 |title=Some Doubts About Folk Jurisprudence: The Case of Proximate Cause |url=https://lawreviewblog.uchicago.edu/2021/08/23/jimenez-jurisprudence/ |journal=University of Chicago Law Review Online}}</ref>

===Predicting philosophical disagreement===
Research suggests that some fundamental philosophical intuitions are related to stable individual differences in personality.  Although there are notable limits,<ref>Doris, J. (2005). Lack of Character. New York: Oxford University Press.</ref> philosophical intuitions and disagreements can be predicted by heritable [[Big Five personality traits]] and their facets.  Extraverts are much more likely to be compatibilists,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Feltz | first1 = A. | last2 = Cokely | first2 = E.T. | year = 2009 | title = Do Judgments about Freedom and Responsibility Depend on Who You Are? Personality Differences in Intuitions about Compatibilism and Incompatibilism | journal = Consciousness and Cognition | volume = 18 | issue = 1| pages = 342–350 | doi=10.1016/j.concog.2008.08.001| pmid = 18805023 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.174.5175 | s2cid = 16953908 }}</ref><ref name="Feltz, A. 2012">{{cite journal | last1 = Feltz | first1 = A. | last2 = Perez | first2 = A. | last3 = Harris | first3 = M. | year = 2012 | title = Free will, causes, and decisions: Individual differences in written reports | journal = Journal of Consciousness Studies | volume = 19 | pages = 166–189 }}</ref> particularly if they are high in "warmth."<ref name="Persistent bias in expert judgments">{{cite journal | last1 = Schulz | first1 = E. | last2 = Cokely | first2 = E.T. | last3 = Feltz | first3 = A. | year = 2011 | title = Persistent bias in expert judgments about free will and moral responsibility: A test of the Expertise Defense | journal = Consciousness and Cognition | volume = 20 | issue = 4| pages = 1722–1731 | doi=10.1016/j.concog.2011.04.007| pmid = 21596586 | s2cid = 42827078 }}</ref> Extraverts show larger biases and different patterns of beliefs in the Knobe side effect cases.<ref name="Feltz, A. 2012"/><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Cokely | first1 = E.T. | last2 = Feltz | first2 = A. | year = 2009 | title = Individual differences, judgment biases, and Theory-of-Mind: Deconstructing the intentional action side effect asymmetry | journal = Journal of Research in Personality | volume = 43 | pages = 18–24 | doi=10.1016/j.jrp.2008.10.007}}</ref> Neuroticism is related to susceptibility to manipulation-style free will arguments.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Feltz | first1 = A | year = 2013 | title = Pereboom and premises: Asking the right questions in the experimental philosophy of free will | journal = Consciousness and Cognition | volume = 22 | issue = 1| pages = 54–63 | doi=10.1016/j.concog.2012.11.007| pmid = 23262252 | s2cid = 32107842 }}</ref> Emotional Stability predicts who will attribute virtues to others.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Cokely | first1 = E.T. | last2 = Feltz | first2 = A. | year = 2011 | title = Virtue in business: Morally better, praiseworthy, trustworthy, and more satisfying | journal = Journal of Organizational Moral Psychology | volume = 2 | pages = 13–26 }}</ref><ref>Feltz. A., & Cokely, E.T. (in press). Virtue or consequences: The folk against Pure Evaluational Internalism. Philosophical Psychology.</ref><ref>Feltz, A., & Cokely, E.T. (2012). The virtues of ignorance. The Review of Philosophy and Psychology, 3, 335-350.</ref> Openness to experience predicts non-objectivist moral intuitions.<ref>Feltz, A., & Cokely, E. T. (2008). The fragmented folk: More evidence of stable individual differences in moral judgments and folk intuitions. In B. C. Love, K. McRae & V. M. Sloutsky (Eds.), Proceedings of the 30th Annual Conference of the Cognitive Science Society (pp. 1771-1776). Austin, TX: Cognitive Science Society.</ref> The link between personality and philosophical intuitions is independent of cognitive abilities, training, education, and expertise.<ref name="Persistent bias in expert judgments"/>  Similar effects have also been found cross-culturally and in different languages including German<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Cokely | first1 = E.T. | last2 = Feltz | first2 = A. | year = 2009 | title = Adaptive variation in judgment and philosophical intuition | journal = Consciousness and Cognition | volume = 18 | pages = 355–357 | doi=10.1016/j.concog.2009.01.001| s2cid = 54233543 }}</ref> and Spanish.

Because the Big Five Personality Traits are highly heritable, some have argued that many contemporary philosophical disputes are likely to persist through the generations. This may mean that some historical philosophical disputes are unlikely to be solved by purely rational, traditional philosophical methods and may require empirical data and experimental philosophy.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Feltz | first1 = A. | last2 = Cokely | first2 = E.T. | year = 2012 | title = The Philosophical Personality Argument | journal = Philosophical Studies | volume = 161 | issue = 2| pages = 227–246 | doi=10.1007/s11098-011-9731-4| s2cid = 170869268 }}</ref>

Additional research suggests that variance in philosophical tendencies is partly explained in part by differences in thinking styles (e.g., the intuitive or reflective reasoning from [[Dual process theory|Dual Process Theory]]),<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Byrd |first1=Nick |title=Reflective reasoning & philosophy |journal=Philosophy Compass |date=2021 |volume=16 |issue=11 |doi=10.1111/phc3.12786 |s2cid=244188923 }}</ref> even among philosophers.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Byrd |first1=Nick |title=Great Minds do not Think Alike: Philosophers' Views Predicted by Reflection, Education, Personality, and Other Demographic Differences |journal=Review of Philosophy and Psychology |date=2022 |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=647–684 |doi=10.1007/s13164-022-00628-y |s2cid=247911367 }}</ref> For example, accepting faulty intuitions on reflection tests has predicted belief in God,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pennycook |first1=Gordon |last2=Ross |first2=Robert M. |last3=Koehler |first3=Derek J. |last4=Fugelsang |first4=Jonathan A. |title=Atheists and Agnostics Are More Reflective than Religious Believers: Four Empirical Studies and a Meta-Analysis |journal=PLOS ONE |date=2016 |volume=11 |issue=4 |article-number=e0153039 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0153039 |pmid=27054566 |pmc=4824409 |bibcode=2016PLoSO..1153039P |doi-access=free }}</ref> and disbelieving that scientific theories are true,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Byrd |first1=Nick |last2=Białek |first2=Michał |title=Your Health vs. My Liberty: Philosophical beliefs dominated reflection and identifiable victim effects when predicting public health recommendation compliance |journal=Cognition |date=2021 |volume=212 |article-number=104649 |doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2021.104649 |pmid=33756152 |pmc=8599940 }}</ref> while correct responses on reflection tests predicts decisions to minimize harm (''a la utilitarianism'') or avoid causing harm (''a la [[deontology]]'') on the trolley problem.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Byrd |first1=Nick |last2=Conway |first2=Paul |title=Not all who ponder count costs: Arithmetic reflection predicts utilitarian tendencies, but logical reflection predicts both deontological and utilitarian tendencies |journal=Cognition |date=2019 |volume=192 |page=103995 |doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2019.06.007 |pmid=31301587 |s2cid=195887195 }}</ref> These data suggest that reasoning habits may be related to philosophical thinking. However, it has been difficult to detect a causal connection between reasoning habit and philosophical thinking.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kneer |first1=Markus |last2=Colaço |first2=David |last3=Alexander |first3=Joshua |last4=Machery |first4=Edouard |title=On second thought: A refutation of the reflection defense |journal=Oxford Studies of Experimental Philosophy |date=2021 |volume=4 |doi=10.1093/oso/9780192856890.003.0010 }}</ref>

==Criticisms==
[[Antti Kauppinen]] (2007) has argued that intuitions will not reflect the content of folk concepts unless they are intuitions of competent concept users who reflect in ideal circumstances and whose judgments reflect the [[semantics]] of their concepts rather than [[pragmatism|pragmatic]] considerations.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kauppinen |first1=Antti |title=The Rise and Fall of Experimental Philosophy |journal=Philosophical Explorations|date=2007 |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=95–118|doi=10.1080/13869790701305871 |s2cid=144989906 |url=https://philarchive.org/rec/KAUTRA }}</ref>  Experimental philosophers are aware of these concerns,<ref>Sinnott-Armstrong, W. [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jp677/Papers/A+C=P.pdf Abstract + Concrete = Paradox] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160111053128/http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jp677/Papers/A+C=P.pdf |date=2016-01-11 }}, 'in Knobe & Nichols (eds.) [https://www.amazon.com/Experimental-Philosophy-Joshua-Knobe/dp/0195323262 ''Experimental Philosophy''], (209-230), 2008.</ref> and acknowledge that they constitute a criticism.

[[Timothy Williamson]] (2008) has argued that we should not construe philosophical evidence as consisting of intuitions.{{Citation needed|date=September 2019}}

Other experimental philosophers have noted that experimental philosophy often fails to meet basic standards of experimental social science. A great deal of the experiments fail to include enough female participants. Analysis of experimental data is often plagued by improper use of statistics, and reliance on [[data mining]]. Others have pointed out that many participants in experimental philosophy studies fail to comprehend the often abstract and complicated materials, and few studies report comprehension checks.<ref>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2019.00215|title = Forget the Folk: Moral Responsibility Preservation Motives and Other Conditions for Compatibilism|journal = Frontiers in Psychology|year = 2019|last1 = Clark|first1 = Cory J.|last2 = Winegard|first2 = Bo M.|last3 = Baumeister|first3 = Roy F.|volume = 10|page = 215|pmid = 30792683|pmc = 6374326|doi-access = free}}</ref> Holtzman argues that a number of experimental philosophers are guilty of [[Cherry picking (fallacy)|suppressing evidence]]. Yet, in lumping together all people's intuitions as those of the 'folk,' critics may be ignoring basic concerns identified by [[Standpoint feminism|standpoint feminists]].

Some research in experimental philosophy is misleading because it examines averaged responses to surveys even though in almost all of the studies in experimental philosophy there have been substantial dissenting minorities. Ignoring individual differences may result in a distorted view of folk intuitions or concepts. This may lead to theoretical and strange fictions about everyday intuitions or concepts that experimental philosophy was designed to avoid akin to creating the fiction that the average human is not a man or a woman, but the average of a man and woman (e.g., the average person has one ovary and one testicle).<ref>Feltz, A., & Cokely, E.T., (in press). Predicting philosophical disagreement. Philosophy Compass.</ref> This criticism is not unique to experimental philosophy but also applies to other sciences such as psychology and chemistry, although experimental philosophers may lack the training to recognize it.

=== Problem of reproducibility ===
In a series of studies published in 2012<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Seyedsayamdost|first=Hamid|date=2012-10-24|title=On Gender and Philosophical Intuition: Failure of Replication and Other Negative Results|doi=10.2139/ssrn.2166447 |ssrn=2166447}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Seyedsayamdost|first=Hamid|date=2012-10-29|title=On Normativity and Epistemic Intuitions: Failure to Detect Differences between Ethnic Groups|doi=10.2139/ssrn.2168530 |ssrn=2168530}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Seyedsayamdost|first=Hamid|date=2012-12-17|title=On Normativity and Epistemic Intuitions: Failure to Detect Differences between Socioeconomic Groups|doi=10.2139/ssrn.2190525 |ssrn=2190525}}</ref> and later peer-reviewed,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Seyedsayamdost|first=Hamid|date=2015-07-04|title=On gender and philosophical intuition: Failure of replication and other negative results|journal=Philosophical Psychology|volume=28|issue=5|pages=642–673|doi=10.1080/09515089.2014.893288|s2cid=144850164 |issn=0951-5089|url=https://philpapers.org/rec/SEYOGA-2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Seyedsayamdost|first=Hamid|date=2015-03-01|journal=Episteme|volume=12|issue=1|pages=95–116|doi=10.1017/epi.2014.27|issn=1742-3600|citeseerx=10.1.1.697.8000|title=On Normativity and Epistemic Intuitions: Failure of Replication|s2cid=146363660 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://etheses.lse.ac.uk/view/creators/Seyedsayamdost=3AHamid=3A=3A.html|title=Items where Author is "Seyedsayamdost, Hamid" - LSE Theses Online|website=etheses.lse.ac.uk|access-date=2017-05-03}}</ref> Hamid Seyedsayamdost showed that some of the most famous results in experimental philosophy were not reproducible. This work gave rise to a focused attention on reproducibility in experimental philosophy. Several philosophers have carried out independent replications and to date all have confirmed Seyedsayamdost's results.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Adleberg|first1=Toni|last2=Thompson|first2=Morgan|last3=Nahmias|first3=Eddy|date=2014-01-01|title=Do Men and Women Have Different Philosophical Intuitions? Further Data|url=https://philpapers.org/rec/ADLDMA|journal=Philosophical Psychology|volume=28|issue=5|pages=615–641|doi=10.1080/09515089.2013.878834|s2cid=2088343 |url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kim|first1=Minsun|last2=Yuan|first2=Yuan|date=2015-01-01|title=No Cross-Cultural Differences in the Gettier Car Case Intuition: A Replication Study of Weinberg Et Al. 2001|url=https://philpapers.org/rec/KIMNCD|journal=Episteme|volume=12|issue=3|pages=355–361|doi=10.1017/epi.2015.17|s2cid=145552371 |url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|url=https://philpapers.org/rec/YUACUO|title=Cross-Cultural Universality of Knowledge Attributions|last1=Yuan|first1=Yuan|last2=Kim|first2=Minun|journal=Review of Philosophy and Psychology }}</ref>

Some of the areas covered in this debate include the instability and malleability of philosophical intuitions, determinism and moral responsibility, cultural diversity, gender differences and socioeconomic diversity. A large amount of research also focused on epistemology as [[Stephen Stich]] argued early on that findings reported by him and co-authors suggested that long practiced methods in philosophy had to be discarded, famously noting that in light of their findings a "reasonable conclusion is that philosophy's 2400 year long infatuation with Plato's method has been a terrible mistake."<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Stich|first=Stephen|date=2001-01-01|title=Plato's Method Meets Cognitive Science|url=https://philpapers.org/rec/STIPMM|journal=Free Inquiry|volume=21|issue=2|pages=36–38}}</ref> Since publication of Seyedsayamdost's papers, Stich and collaborators have reversed their research direction on this question.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Machery|first1=Edouard|last2=Stich|first2=Stephen|last3=Rose|first3=David|last4=Chatterjee|first4=Amita|last5=Karasawa|first5=Kaori|last6=Struchiner|first6=Noel|last7=Sirker|first7=Smita|last8=Usui|first8=Naoki|last9=Hashimoto|first9=Takaaki|date=2015-01-01|title=Gettier Across Cultures|url=https://philpapers.org/rec/MACGAC-4|journal=Noûs|volume=50|issue=4}}</ref> The reason for these problems in experimental philosophy is not entirely clear, although a parallel with experimental psychology has been suggested.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/win2016/entries/experimental-moral/|title=The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy|last1=Alfano|first1=Mark|last2=Loeb|first2=Don|chapter=Experimental Moral Philosophy |date=2016-01-01|publisher=Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University|editor-last=Zalta|editor-first=Edward N.|edition=Winter 2016}}</ref>

At least one recent study, in which a team attempted to replicate various influential studies in experimental philosophy studies, found that roughly 70% of them could be replicated. The reasons for the discrepancy with Seyedsayamdost's original study are not yet known.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Estimating the Reproducibility of Experimental Philosophy|journal=Review of Philosophy and Psychology|doi=10.1007/s13164-018-0400-9|year=2018|last1=Cova|first1=Florian|last2=Strickland|first2=Brent|last3=Abatista|first3=Angela|last4=Allard|first4=Aurélien|last5=Andow|first5=James|last6=Attie|first6=Mario|last7=Beebe|first7=James|last8=Berniūnas|first8=Renatas|last9=Boudesseul|first9=Jordane|last10=Colombo|first10=Matteo|last11=Cushman|first11=Fiery|last12=Diaz|first12=Rodrigo|last13=n'Djaye Nikolai Van Dongen|first13=Noah|last14=Dranseika|first14=Vilius|last15=Earp|first15=Brian D.|last16=Torres|first16=Antonio Gaitán|last17=Hannikainen|first17=Ivar|last18=Hernández-Conde|first18=José V.|last19=Hu|first19=Wenjia|last20=Jaquet|first20=François|last21=Khalifa|first21=Kareem|last22=Kim|first22=Hanna|last23=Kneer|first23=Markus|last24=Knobe|first24=Joshua|last25=Kurthy|first25=Miklos|last26=Lantian|first26=Anthony|last27=Liao|first27=Shen-yi|last28=Machery|first28=Edouard|last29=Moerenhout|first29=Tania|last30=Mott|first30=Christian|volume=12 |pages=9–44 |display-authors=29|url=https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/160900/1/Kneer_EstimatingTheReproducibilityOf_2018.pdf|hdl=10261/221695|s2cid=149934794 |hdl-access=free}}</ref>

==References==
{{reflist|colwidth=30em}}

* Buckwalter, W. (2010). "Knowledge Isn't Closed on Saturday: A Study in Ordinary Language", ''Review of Philosophy and Psychology'' (formerly ''European Review of Philosophy''), special issue on Psychology and Experimental Philosophy ed. by Edouard Machery, Tania Lombrozo, & Joshua Knobe, 1 (3):395-406. [https://wfs.gc.cuny.edu/JBuckwalter/www/Buckwalter-KOS-Final.pdf (link)]{{dead link|date=September 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}
* {{cite journal |last1=Donelson |first1=R. |last2=Hannikainen |first2=I. |editor1-last=Lombrozo |editor1-first=Tania |editor2-last=Knobe |editor2-first=Joshua |editor3-last=Nichols |editor3-first=Shaun |title=Fuller and the Folk: The Inner Morality of Law Revisited |journal=Oxford Studies in Experimental Philosophy |date=2019 |volume=3 |ssrn=3283952 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Feltz | first1 = A. | last2 = Zarpentine | first2 = C. | year = 2010 | title = Do You Know More When It Matters Less? | url = http://faculty.schreiner.edu/adfeltz/Papers/Know%20more.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100712235035/http://faculty.schreiner.edu/adfeltz/Papers/Know%20more.pdf | archive-date = 2010-07-12 | journal = Philosophical Psychology | volume = 23 | issue = 5| pages = 683–706 | doi=10.1080/09515089.2010.514572| citeseerx = 10.1.1.174.4685 | s2cid = 15444124 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Kauppinen | first1 = A | year = 2007 | title = The Rise and Fall of Experimental Philosophy | url = http://www.helsinki.fi/~amkauppi/phil/The_Rise_and_Fall_of_Experimental_Philosophy.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20110805040203/http://www.helsinki.fi/~amkauppi/phil/The_Rise_and_Fall_of_Experimental_Philosophy.pdf | archive-date = 2011-08-05 | journal = Philosophical Explorations | volume = 10 | issue = 2| pages = 95–118 | doi=10.1080/13869790701305871| s2cid = 144989906 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Knobe | first1 = J | year = 2003a | title = Intentional action and side effects in ordinary language | url = http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/Side-Effect.pdf | journal = Analysis | volume = 63 | issue = 3| pages = 190–193 | doi=10.1093/analys/63.3.190}}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Knobe | first1 = J | year = 2003b | title = Intentional action in folk psychology: An experimental investigation | url = http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/IntentionSkill.pdf | journal = Philosophical Psychology | volume = 16 | issue = 2| pages = 309–324 | doi=10.1080/09515080307771| citeseerx = 10.1.1.318.5898 | s2cid = 12326690 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Knobe | first1 = J | year = 2004a | title = Intention, Intentional Action and Moral Considerations | journal = Analysis | volume = 64 | issue = 2| pages = 181–187 | doi=10.1093/analys/64.2.181}}
* Knobe, J. (2004b). "What is Experimental Philosophy?" ''The Philosophers' Magazine'', 28. [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/ExperimentalPhilosophy.pdf (link)]
* {{cite journal | last1 = Knobe | first1 = J | year = 2007 | title = Experimental Philosophy and Philosophical Significance | url = http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/phil-significance.pdf | journal = Philosophical Explorations | volume = 10 | issue = 2| pages = 119–122 | doi=10.1080/13869790701305905| s2cid = 171040750 }}
* Knobe, J. & Nichols, S. (2008). ''Experimental philosophy''. Oxford University Press, USA. [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfUeuA8-80AC (link)].
* Knobe, J. and Jesse Prinz. (2008). "Intuitions about Consciousness: Experimental Studies". ''Phenomenology and Cognitive Science''.[http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/consciousness.pdf (link)]
* Kripke, S. (1980). ''Naming and Necessity''. Harvard University Press.
* [[Christoph Luetge|Luetge, C.]], Rusch, H., Uhl, M. (2014). ''Experimental Ethics: Toward an Empirical Moral Philosophy''. Palgrave Macmillan. 
* {{cite journal | last1 = Machery | first1 = E. | last2 = Mallon | first2 = R. | last3 = Nichols | first3 = S. | last4 = Stich | first4 = S. | year = 2004 | title = Semantics, Cross-Cultural Style | journal = Cognition | volume = 92 | issue = 3| pages = B1–B12 | doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2003.10.003| pmid = 15019555 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.174.5119 | s2cid = 15074526 }}
* {{cite journal | doi = 10.1007/s13164-009-0014-3 | pmid=22558061 | pmc=3339025 | volume=1 | issue=2 | title=Practical Interests, Relevant Alternatives, and Knowledge Attributions: an Empirical Study | year=2010 | journal=Review of Philosophy and Psychology | pages=265–273 | last1 = May | first1 = Joshua | last2 = Sinnott-Armstrong | first2 = Walter | last3 = Hull | first3 = Jay G. | last4 = Zimmerman | first4 = Aaron}}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Nichols | first1 = S | s2cid = 15873321 | year = 2002 | title = How Psychopaths Threaten Moral Rationalism: Is It Irrational to Be Amoral? | journal = The Monist | volume = 85 | issue = 2| pages = 285–304 | doi=10.5840/monist200285210}}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Nichols | first1 = S | year = 2004 | title = After Objectivity: An Empirical Study of Moral Judgment | journal = Philosophical Psychology | volume = 17 | pages = 5–28 | doi=10.1080/0951508042000202354| citeseerx = 10.1.1.404.9797 | s2cid = 15114919 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Nichols | first1 = S. | last2 = Folds-Bennett | first2 = T. | year = 2003 | title = Are Children Moral Objectivists? Children's Judgments about Moral and Response-Dependent Properties | journal = Cognition | volume = 90 | issue = 2| pages = B23–32 | doi=10.1016/s0010-0277(03)00160-4| pmid = 14599758 | s2cid = 2292888 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Nichols | first1 = S. | last2 = Knobe | first2 = J. | year = 2007 | title = Moral Responsibility and Determinism: The Cognitive Science of Folk Intuitions | url = http://www.unc.edu/~knobe/Nichols-Knobe.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20091211132936/http://www.unc.edu/~knobe/Nichols-Knobe.pdf | archive-date = 2009-12-11 | journal = Noûs | volume = 41 | issue = 4| pages = 663–685 | doi=10.1111/j.1468-0068.2007.00666.x| citeseerx = 10.1.1.175.1091 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Sandis | first1 = C | year = 2010 | title = The Experimental Turn and Ordinary Language | url = http://commons.pacificu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1364&context=eip | journal = Essays in Philosophy | volume = 11 | issue = 2| pages = 181–196 | doi = 10.5840/eip20101125 }}
* Schaffer, J. & Knobe, J. (2012). "Contrastive Knowledge Surveyed". ''Noûs''. [http://philpapers.org/rec/SCHCKS-2 (link)], [http://www.jonathanschaffer.org/surveyed.pdf (link)]
* {{cite journal | last1 = Sytsma | first1 = J. | last2 = Machery | first2 = E. | year = 2009 | title = How to Study Folk Intuitions about Consciousness | url = http://www.unc.edu/~knobe/Sytsma-Machery.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090124143140/http://www.unc.edu/~knobe/Sytsma-Machery.pdf | archive-date = 2009-01-24 | journal = Philosophical Psychology | volume =  22| pages = 21–35 | doi=10.1080/09515080802703653| citeseerx = 10.1.1.494.4190 | s2cid = 16765924 }}
* {{cite journal | last1 = Weinberg | first1 = J. | last2 = Nichols | first2 = S. | last3 = Stich | first3 = S. | year = 2001 | title = Normativity and Epistemic Intuitions | journal = Philosophical Topics | volume = 29 | pages = 429–460 | doi=10.5840/philtopics2001291/217| citeseerx = 10.1.1.174.4748 }}
* Williamson, T. (2008). ''The Philosophy of Philosophy''. Wiley-Blackwell.
* Spicer, F. (2009). "The X-philes: Review of Experimental Philosophy, edited by Knobe and Nichols". ''The Philosophers' Magazine'' (44): 107. Retrieved 2009-01-08. [https://web.archive.org/web/20090116073950/http://www.philosophersnet.com/ (link)]

==External links==
* {{PhilPapers|category|experimental-philosophy}} (Edited by Wesley Buckwalter).
* {{InPho|idea|648}}
* [https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/experimental-philosophy/ Experimental Philosophy] at [[Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy]]. 
* [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/ExperimentalPhilosophy.html The Experimental Philosophy Page]
* [http://philosophycommons.typepad.com/xphi/ The Experimental Philosophy Blog] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150707120923/http://philosophycommons.typepad.com/xphi/ |date=2015-07-07 }} - with several prominent contemporary philosophers as contributors.
** (As of July 2009, The Experimental Philosophy Page and Blog list around 120 different contributors who are actively involved with research in experimental philosophy.)
* [http://pantheon.yale.edu/~jk762/lab/ 'Yale's Experimental Philosophy Lab (EPL)]
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20100610082824/http://x-phi.org/ Experimental Philosophy Society (XPS)]
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20100417204712/http://www.epl.web.arizona.edu/home.html Arizona's Experimental Philosophy Lab]
* [http://www.indiana.edu/~eel/ Indiana's Experimental Epistemology Lab]
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20100713002014/http://faculty.schreiner.edu/adfeltz/Lab/index.html Schreiner's Behavioral Philosophy Lab]
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20100701010002/http://www.bris.ac.uk/philosophy/projects/exp_phil Bristol's Experimental Philosophy Page]
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20100620062220/http://eerg.buffalo.edu/ Buffalo's Experimental Epistemology Research Group (EERG)]
